input_dict.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.
input_dict.base_prompt:
Given inputs question (string) and passages (list of strings), produce a single textual output to populate the field "summary". The "summary" output must include the following labeled sections and follow these rules exactly.

1) Format
- Begin with: [[ ## reasoning ## ]]
- End the reasoning block and then include: [[ ## summary ## ]]
- The final output may optionally end with: [[ ## completed ## ]]

2) Reasoning section (required)
- For each passage i, include a concise mapping that:
  - Quotes or paraphrases the exact supporting text from Passage [i] (use quotation marks or exact substring).
  - Explicitly maps each quoted fact to the entity and scope it supports (e.g., "Passage [3] → ENTITY: Dan Boren; SCOPE: biographical birth date"; or "Passage [1] → ENTITY: Ilha (Santana); SCOPE: parish-level population 2011").
  - If a passage contains only lower-level, local, or different-scope facts that are NOT sufficient to answer the question (e.g., parish populations when the question asks for archipelago total), explicitly state that and refuse to treat them as the final answer.

- After mapping passages, state whether the exact target fact required by the question is present in the provided passages:
  - If present: identify the exact sentence(s) and supporting-text pattern matched (literal pattern required, e.g., "ENTITY_NAME (born <date>)" or "The population in 2011 was <number>"). Cite passage indices that contain that exact supporting text.
  - If not present: explain precisely which fact is missing and why the existing passage facts are insufficient (cite passage indices).

3) If missing: prioritized next-hop retrieval plan (required when exact supporting text is absent)
- Name the target entity to retrieve (canonical/title-first form) and give one-sentence rationale linking it to the question (use the bridging passage index).
- Provide at least three disambiguating short query variants (each a short search phrase). At least one variant must begin with the canonical/title-first suggestion. Include scope/context tokens (e.g., "birth date", "2011 population", "archipelago", "U.S. Representative", "politician", location).
- Specify the exact supporting-text pattern required from the retrieved document that will permit a final answer (literal pattern, e.g., "Dan Boren (born <date>)", or "The population of Madeira in 2011 was <number>", or "X (born YYYY-MM-DD)").
- Provide the exact bridging phrase(s) the follow-up query should include to connect current passages to the target (e.g., "Dan Boren" + "Oklahoma's 2nd Congressional District" or "Ilha (Santana) → Madeira").
- Include a short reasoning subsection that cites the passage indices showing why a second hop is needed (one or two sentences).

4) If present: final-answer extraction
- Extract the final answer verbatim from the supporting sentence(s) in the passages.
- State the exact supporting-text pattern matched.
- Cite the passage index(es) that contain the supporting text.

5) Summary section (one line)
- Provide a one-line summary that is either:
  - The cited answer followed by its supporting passage index(es), OR
  - A concise retrieval plan statement that names the target entity and says "retrieve [target]" (and optionally lists the top query variant), if a second hop is needed.
- Do not include any new facts not present in the passages or the retrieval plan.

6) Constraints and behavior
- Never answer from memory. If the exact required supporting text is not in the provided passages, do not invent or guess an answer—emit the retrieval plan described above.
- Be concise and prioritized: when providing query variants, order them by priority.
- Always include passage indices in reasoning to justify conclusions.
- Generalize this behavior to any question type (dates, populations, roles, totals, comparisons, etc.).

delta_p.trigger: If, during the reasoning section, you establish that Passage [2] (the 'Orientalism' passage) asserts that 'Orientalist painting' was a 19th‑century academic art specialism, but no passage contains a named academic who both (a) is described as having that specialism and (b) has a clear university appointment phrase (e.g., 'taught at', 'professor at', 'was a lecturer at'), then apply the following change.
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep all existing required formatting and mapping behavior: quote or paraphrase supporting text per passage, map each quoted fact to entity+scope, state whether the exact target fact is present, and follow the existing 'If missing' structure in section 3 of the prompt.
delta_p.change_rule: When the trigger condition holds, extend the 'If missing' prioritized next-hop retrieval plan to explicitly (a) name the retrieval target as the candidate listed in any system-provided 'missing candidate' or 'flagged candidate' metadata (if such a name is available in agent inputs); otherwise name the generic target as 'the academic associated with Orientalist painting (candidate name unknown)'; (b) give a one-sentence rationale linking that target to the question and cite Passage [2]; (c) provide at least three prioritized, disambiguating short query variants. Require that one query variant begins with the canonical/title-first suggestion if a canonical name is available in inputs; if no canonical name is available, the first query must be a high-priority descriptive query beginning with the phrase: "Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting" followed by university/biography scope tokens. Example query forms to follow this rule: 1) "[Canonical Name]" + "university" or "Wikipedia" (only if canonical name provided in inputs), 2) "Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting" + "taught at" OR "university" OR "Wikipedia", 3) "Biography" + "academic" + "Orientalist painting" + "taught". (Do not invent or inject any specific person name unless it already appears in agent inputs.) (d) Specify the exact supporting-text pattern required from the retrieved document that will permit a final answer, e.g., one of the literal patterns: "NAME taught at <University>", "NAME is a professor of <field> at <University>", or "NAME was a lecturer at <University>". (e) Provide explicit bridging phrase(s) that the follow-up query should include to connect the current passages to the target, for example: "Orientalist painting" + "special interests" or "Orientalist painting" + "academic interests", and cite Passage [2]. (f) Include a brief (one- or two-sentence) reasoning line that cites Passage [2] to justify why the second hop is needed.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not fabricate or insert any specific person name, university name, or final answer that is not already present in the visible agent inputs. Avoid adding long new factual claims or changing the required output structure. Avoid producing multiple-hop plans beyond the single prioritized next-hop plan required here.
delta_p.output_contract: When applied, the agent's output must still follow the original numbered structure. In section 3 (the 'If missing' plan) the agent must now include: (1) Target entity name as described (either the canonical name from inputs or the generic placeholder), (2) One-sentence rationale linked to Passage [2], (3) At least three ordered query variants (obeying the canonical-first rule when a name is available), (4) The exact literal supporting-text pattern(s) required to accept a retrieved document as final evidence (one of the specified patterns), (5) Exact bridging phrase(s) to include in the follow-up query, and (6) A one- or two-sentence justification citing Passage [2]. All other prompt output constraints remain in force.
delta_p.why_local_transition: The base prompt already requires detecting whether the exact academic+university fact is present and, if missing, producing a next-hop plan. This edit narrows and clarifies how to construct that next-hop plan specifically for cases where 'Orientalist painting' is confirmed in Passage [2] but the named academic/university pairing is absent: it enforces a concise, prioritized retrieval target naming rule, canonical-first query ordering (only when a canonical name is available in inputs), exact supporting-text acceptance patterns, and explicit bridging phrases. This is the minimal behavioral change needed to shift from a generic 'retrieve' instruction to a concrete, rule-compliant next-hop retrieval plan without adding any new factual claims.

delta_p.trigger: When the reasoning section determines that the passages establish 'Orientalist painting' as a 19th‑century academic-art specialism but do NOT contain a named academic plus university appointment (i.e., the exact 'X taught at <University>' text is absent).
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep the existing output format and all required mapping behavior exactly (the [[ ## reasoning ## ]] and [[ ## summary ## ]] blocks, per‑passage quoted/paraphrased mappings, citation of passage indices, and the constraint to refuse final answers not literally supported by passages).
delta_p.change_rule: If the trigger fires, append a concise next‑hop retrieval plan tailored to finding an academic's biography/staff page and university appointment. Do this as follows, smallest change first: (a) If a candidate academic name already appears in the provided passages or any visible input, set that canonical name as the 'target entity' and produce a canonical-first retrieval target plus three prioritized short query variants that emphasize biography/staff pages and university affiliation. (b) If no candidate name appears in the inputs, set the target description generically as 'academic who specialized in Orientalist painting' and produce at least three prioritized short query variants that focus on biographies/staff pages/university appointments (examples: "academic who specialized in Orientalist painting biography", "Orientalist painting scholar university profile", "Orientalism 'biography' 'taught at' site:wikipedia"). In all cases explicitly state the exact supporting-text pattern required to accept a retrieved document (literal pattern required), and list the bridging phrase(s) the follow-up query should include (e.g., "Orientalist painting" + "biography" or "Orientalist painting" + "taught at").
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do NOT invent or insert any specific candidate names, example academics, or long literal spans that do not already appear in the visible passages. Avoid adding extra illustrative examples or speculative facts. Do not change the required summary format or output fields. Do not produce a final answer from memory.
delta_p.output_contract: When missing, the 'If missing: prioritized next-hop retrieval plan' must include: (1) a single target entity name if and only if that name already appears in the visible inputs; otherwise a concise target description 'academic who specialized in Orientalist painting'; (2) at least three prioritized short query variants (first variant must be canonical/title-first if a canonical name is available; otherwise first variant should begin with the target description); (3) the exact supporting-text literal pattern required to permit a final answer (for this question: a sentence matching either "NAME taught at <University>" or "NAME is (a|the) .* at <University>" or the exact substring "taught at <University>"); (4) bridging phrase(s) the follow-up query should include (e.g., "Orientalist painting" + "biography" or "Orientalist painting" + "taught at"); and (5) a one- to two-sentence justification citing the passage index(es) that show why a second hop is required. Do not add any facts not present in the passages.
delta_p.why_local_transition: This narrow edit directs the agent to target biography/staff‑page queries that will yield the explicit 'taught at <University>' supporting text required by the base prompt, while preserving the existing mapping and refusal rules and avoiding invented candidate names.

delta_p.trigger: If, after mapping passages, the Reasoning section shows that 'Orientalist painting' (or the phrase 'Orientalist' / 'Orientalist painting') is identified as the bridge specialism but the passages do not contain a sentence that states which university the relevant academic taught at.
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep all existing formatting and mapping requirements exactly (the [[ ## reasoning ## ]] / [[ ## summary ## ]] blocks, per-passage quoted/paraphrased mappings, explicit statements about whether the exact supporting text is present, and the requirement to include passage indices). Preserve any evidence already extracted from passages; do not remove previously extracted quotations or mappings.
delta_p.change_rule: When the trigger fires, augment the 'missing' branch of the retrieval plan: (a) name the retrieval target using a canonical, descriptive label 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting' (do not invent an actual personal name), and add one-sentence rationale linking that target to the bridging passage identified in the reasoning (refer to the passage index found by the reasoning). (b) Provide at least three prioritized short query variants (ordered) where the first variant begins with the canonical/title-first phrase. Each variant must be short and include a scope token (examples to use exactly as query text): 1) 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting biography' 2) 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting university' 3) 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting Wikipedia'. (c) Instruct the follow-up retrieval to check for sections or lines about academic appointments/professorships on biography or staff pages. (d) Specify the exact supporting-text pattern required to permit a final answer: one of the literal patterns must appear in the retrieved document, for example 'NAME taught at <University>' or 'NAME is a Professor at <University>' (the agent should list these literal patterns verbatim as required match strings). (e) Supply bridging phrase(s) the follow-up query should include to connect current passages to the target, e.g. the exact phrase 'Orientalist painting' plus the token 'academic' (the agent should concatenate the bridging phrase with the canonical target in queries).
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not invent, assert, or prioritize any specific personal name as the target; do not produce or imply a final answer; do not copy any long literal span that is present only in later/target internal states. Avoid adding extra retrieval variants that introduce new entities or locations not present in the visible passages. Avoid changing the required output format or removing the passage-index citations.
delta_p.output_contract: When triggered, the summary output must include an explicit retrieval plan that contains: (1) the target entity named exactly as 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting'; (2) a one-sentence rationale linking that target to the specific passage index cited by the Reasoning section (use the same index numbers already present in the Reasoning); (3) at least three prioritized short query variants, with the first beginning 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting' and each containing a scope token ('biography', 'university', 'Wikipedia'); (4) the exact supporting-text literal match patterns required to accept a retrieved document as final evidence, listed verbatim (for example: 'NAME taught at <University>' and 'NAME is a Professor at <University>'); and (5) explicit bridging phrase(s) to include in queries, at minimum the exact token 'Orientalist painting' plus 'academic'. The rest of the summary must still follow the base prompt's sections and rules.
delta_p.why_local_transition: This is a minimal, local refinement: it only alters how the agent formulates the next-hop retrieval plan (narrowing and ordering query text and declaring required literal supporting patterns) when the bridge is 'Orientalist painting' and the university is missing. It preserves the required mapping format and all evidence already extracted, and avoids introducing any new personal-name assertions.

delta_p.trigger: If the agent's current_output proposes a retrieval for an academic tied to the phrase "Orientalist painting" (i.e., the output contains the substrings "retrieve" and "Orientalist painting" and references an "academic" or "Academic who specialized"), apply this delta.
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep every existing requirement from the base prompt: preserve the exact reasoning/summary format blocks, the per-passage quotation-and-mapping behavior, the explicit statement about whether the exact supporting text is present, and the constraint not to answer from memory. Preserve any candidate-name mentions or tentative candidates already present in the agent's own reasoning text.
delta_p.change_rule: When the trigger applies, modify only the next-hop / 'missing' subsection behavior: (a) If the agent's reasoning already proposes a candidate academic name, require the retrieval-plan to present the target entity in canonical/title-first form (placeholder: <CANDIDATE>) at the start of the retrieval plan line (e.g., '<CANDIDATE>' followed by one-sentence rationale). (b) Always supply at least three prioritized short query variants; the first variant must begin with the canonical/title-first form (i.e., start with '<CANDIDATE>' if a candidate is present; otherwise start with the most specific concise phrase possible, e.g., 'Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting') and the variants must include at least one that explicitly uses the words 'taught at' or 'university' and at least one that uses 'biography' or 'staff profile'. (c) Specify the exact supporting-text literal pattern required from the retrieved document that will permit a final answer; express this pattern using the same placeholder <CANDIDATE>, for example: 'Exact supporting-text pattern required: "<CANDIDATE> taught at <University>" or "<CANDIDATE> is (a) professor (of|at) <University>".' (d) Provide bridging phrase(s) that combine the visible passage phrase 'Orientalist painting' with the candidate placeholder, e.g., '"Orientalist painting" + <CANDIDATE>'.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not convert the retrieval plan into a final answer; do not invent or assert any university or affiliation. Do not insert any sample-specific proper name that does not already appear in the visible agent inputs. Avoid adding extra sections beyond those required by the base prompt; keep the change minimal and focused on clarifying retrieval phrasing and supporting-text pattern.
delta_p.output_contract: When applied, the agent's produced 'missing' / next-hop retrieval plan must (1) name the target entity in canonical/title-first form at the plan's start if the agent already suggested a candidate (use placeholder <CANDIDATE> if needed), (2) list at least three prioritized short query variants (first begins with the canonical/title-first form or the most specific phrase possible), (3) include at least one query variant containing the tokens 'taught at' or 'university' and at least one containing 'biography' or 'staff profile', (4) state an exact literal supporting-text pattern required for final answer extraction using the placeholder <CANDIDATE> (for example: '"<CANDIDATE> taught at <University>"' or '"<CANDIDATE> is a professor at <University>"'), and (5) include bridging phrase(s) that explicitly link the visible passage phrase 'Orientalist painting' to the candidate (e.g., '"Orientalist painting" + <CANDIDATE>'). All other base-prompt formatting and mapping obligations remain unchanged.
delta_p.why_local_transition: The source summary already points to a next-hop candidate and a retrieval direction; this small, local prompt edit enforces canonical-first query phrasing and a precise required supporting-text pattern so the next-hop retrieval is focused on university affiliation evidence and can be directly used to extract the final answer. It does not change passage-mapping behavior or invent facts—only tightens the retrieval-plan output format to match the next-step need.

visible_agent_inputs.question: This specialism of 19th-century academic art was among the special interests of this academic who taught at which University?
passages: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University | Tilburg University offers an intensive three-year program in Liberal Arts and Sciences, which combines a broad and interdisciplinary education with in-depth specialization.  The distinctive focus of Tilburg University’s Liberal Arts and Sciences program is on Europe in a globalizing world.  Both historical and current societal key issues are tackled in the course of this international program, taught entirely in English.  Students are trained to approach complex issues from different perspectives and from a variety of academic disciplines.  To achieve this intellectual flexibility, lectures are team-taught by lecturers from different academic backgrounds and research traditions.  This method of instruction is unique to Tilburg University.

Edward E. Baptist | Edward E. Baptist (born 1970) is an American academic and writer.  He is a Professor of History at Cornell University, located in Ithaca, New York, where he specializes in the history of the 19th-century United States, and particularly the South.  Thematically, he has been interested in the history of capitalism, and has also been interested in digital humanities methodologies.  He is the author of numerous books.

Orientalism | Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).  These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.  In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.

Harrogate Tutorial College | Harrogate Tutorial College in Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England is a small co-educational independent school.  HTC specialises in preparing students for university and teaches GCSE and A-level qualifications in tutorial classes.  The college's admissions policy and style of teaching has made it a popular choice among pupils of British public schools who have not met the often restrictive academic requirements of their schools to proceed to A-level.  Attracting many overseas students wishing to enroll at British universities, the college also provides English language courses which are often taught in combination with academic subjects.  Special preparation is offered to Oxford and Cambridge applicants and 90% of HTC students secure places at university.

Master of Arts in Special Education | The Master of Arts in Special Education is a postgraduate academic master's degree awarded by universities in many countries.  This degree designed to develop the capabilities and resources of educators of children who require Special education, such as students with an Autism spectrum disorder.

Seth Evans | Seth Evans is a scholar and educator who specializes in the Abhidhamma Pitaka (abhidhammapiṭaka) and the Visuddhimagga.  Especially interested in the phenomenological aspects of Buddhist psychology.  Academic focus is primarily on Buddhist ontology, Buddhist metaphysics, and Buddhist epistemology.  He did his graduate studies in Buddhist theory as a Theravada monk at Mahachulalongkornrajavidyalaya University.  As a monk, he taught meditation at Wat Prayong where he completed five vassa.

Brazilian academic art | Brazilian Academic art was a major art style in Brazil from the early 19th century to the early 20th century, based on European academic art and produced on official institutions of professional art education.
visible_agent_inputs.current_output: retrieve "Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting" — top query: "Academic who specialized in Orientalist painting taught at university"

input_dict.output_requirement: Return one summary_1 string. Summarize the visible hop1 passages and expose a useful second-hop retrieval direction.

request.rendered_prompt:
You are executing one agent in a fixed HotpotQA multi-agent pipeline.

Use the unchanged base instruction p together with the complete ORDERED
list of local prompt deltas. The deltas are supplemental behavior guidance
for this sample; they are not outputs and must not be quoted mechanically.

This is a direct delta-context execution probe:
- Do not rewrite, aggregate, or synthesize a new prompt p'.
- Apply every delta in order to the visible inputs.
- Judge success only by producing the endpoint agent output.
- Do not attempt to reproduce or discuss intermediate states.
- Do not infer hidden information from the existence of the deltas.
- Use only the visible agent inputs as factual evidence.
- Never output gold/oracle labels, evaluation metrics, or hidden support.
- Preserve the agent's normal input/output contract.

Agent:
summary1

Agent role:
Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

Base prompt p:

1) Format

2) Reasoning section (required)

  - Quotes or paraphrases the exact supporting text from Passage [i] (use quotation marks or exact substring).
  - Explicitly maps each quoted fact to the entity and scope it supports (e.g., "Passage [3] → ENTITY: Dan Boren; SCOPE: biographical birth date"; or "Passage [1] → ENTITY: Ilha (Santana); SCOPE: parish-level population 2011").
  - If a passage contains only lower-level, local, or different-scope facts that are NOT sufficient to answer the question (e.g., parish populations when the question asks for archipelago total), explicitly state that and refuse to treat them as the final answer.

- After mapping passages, state whether the exact target fact required by the question is present in the provided passages:
  - If present: identify the exact sentence(s) and supporting-text pattern matched (literal pattern required, e.g., "ENTITY_NAME (born <date>)" or "The population in 2011 was <number>"). Cite passage indices that contain that exact supporting text.
  - If not present: explain precisely which fact is missing and why the existing passage facts are insufficient (cite passage indices).

3) If missing: prioritized next-hop retrieval plan (required when exact supporting text is absent)
- Name the target entity to retrieve (canonical/title-first form) and give one-sentence rationale linking it to the question (use the bridging passage index).
- Provide at least three disambiguating short query variants (each a short search phrase). At least one variant must begin with the canonical/title-first suggestion. Include scope/context tokens (e.g., "birth date", "2011 population", "archipelago", "U.S. Representative", "politician", location).
- Specify the exact supporting-text pattern required from the retrieved document that will permit a final answer (literal pattern, e.g., "Dan Boren (born <date>)", or "The population of Madeira in 2011 was <number>", or "X (born YYYY-MM-DD)").
- Provide the exact bridging phrase(s) the follow-up query should include to connect current passages to the target (e.g., "Dan Boren" + "Oklahoma's 2nd Congressional District" or "Ilha (Santana) → Madeira").
- Include a short reasoning subsection that cites the passage indices showing why a second hop is needed (one or two sentences).

4) If present: final-answer extraction

- State the exact supporting-text pattern matched.
- Cite the passage index(es) that contain the supporting text.

5) Summary section (one line)

  - The cited answer followed by its supporting passage index(es), OR

- Do not include any new facts not present in the passages or the retrieval plan.

6) Constraints and behavior
- Never answer from memory. If the exact required supporting text is not in the provided passages, do not invent or guess an answer—emit the retrieval plan described above.
- Be concise and prioritized: when providing query variants, order them by priority.
- Always include passage indices in reasoning to justify conclusions.
- Generalize this behavior to any question type (dates, populations, roles, totals, comparisons, etc.).
